text: Following He and Glass (2019), we construct a list of malicious targets to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences from that list. However, beyond preventing the model from producing targets in a particular list, it is also important to examine whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. Accordingly, we also create a test target list containing targets that are similar to, but different from, those in the training list in order to evaluate generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is furthermore of interest to investigate whether using a larger number of malicious targets for negative training can reduce the hit rate on the test list. To this end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model on the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), using a model with the same architecture as described in Section 2. The paraphrase model is then used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the unaugmented training list is first used for negative training, and then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets, respectively (i.e., 1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Examples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2. The same training, augmented training, and test lists are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between the training lists, whether augmented or not, and the test list.
source_text: Following (He and Glass, 2019) , a list of malicious targets are created to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences in the list. However, in addition to prevent the model from generating targets in a specific list, it is also important to check whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. So, a test target list which contains similar but different targets from the training list are also created to test generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is also interesting to investigate whether using more malicious targets for negative training can lower the hit rate on the test list. Towards that end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model using the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017) , with a model of the same structure as described in Section 2. Then, the paraphrase model is used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in the training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the training list without augmentation is first used for negative training, then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets respectively (1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Samples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2 . The same training, augmented training and test list are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between training lists (augmented or not) and the test list.

text: Policy learning for chitchat generation To mitigate the degeneration problem of word-level policy models (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b), prior studies separate policy learning from response generation, and then adopt utterance-level latent variables or keywords (Yao et al., 2018) as RL actions to steer response generation. In this work, we examine how prior dialog-transition information can be exploited to support dialog policy learning. Knowledge aware conversation generation There is increasing interest in leveraging knowledge bases to produce more informative responses (Dinan et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Moghe et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). In this work, instead of an external knowledge base, we employ a dialog-modeling oriented graph constructed from dialog corpora to facilitate multi-turn policy learning, rather than to improve dialog informativeness.
source_text: Policy learning for chitchat generation To address the degeneration issue of word-level policy models (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b) , previous works decouple policy learning from response generation, and then use utterance-level latent variables or keywords (Yao et al., 2018) as RL actions to guide response generation. In this work, we investigate how to use prior dialog-transition information to facilitate dialog policy learning. Knowledge aware conversation generation There are growing interests in leveraging knowledge bases for generation of more informative responses (Dinan et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Moghe et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020) . In this work, we employ a dialog-modeling oriented graph built from dialog corpora, instead of a external knowledge base, in order to facilitate multi-turn policy learning, instead of dialog informativeness improvement.

text: A recent method proposed by Wu et al. (2019) introduces an auxiliary loss that verifies whether the object words appear in the anticipated system response of a task-oriented dialogue system. It would be worthwhile to incorporate this auxiliary loss into the proposed method. By contrast, the REINFORCE algorithm (Williams, 1992) used in this paper is more general than that of Wu et al. (2019), since it can also accommodate other metrics, such as BLEU.
source_text: A recent method in (Wu et al., 2019) defines an auxiliary loss that checks if the object words exist in the expected system response of a task-oriented dialogue system. It would be interesting to apply this auxiliary loss in the proposed method. On the other hand, the REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) algorithm applied in this paper is more general than (Wu et al., 2019) to incorporate other metrics, such as BLEU.

text: We employ the sentence-pair classifier of Parikh et al. (2016), here referred to as the DA model. It computes attention-based word alignments over the input pair (the premise and hypothesis, or in our case question q_i and response r_ij) and combines them through feedforward networks. In addition to standard vector embeddings, we also evaluate the DA model with contextualized ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), using the implementation provided in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017).
source_text: We use the sentence pair classifier from (Parikh et al., 2016) , referred as the DA model. It finds attention based wordalignment of the input pair (premise and hypothesis, in our case question q i and response r ij ) and aggregates it using feedforward networks. Apart from standard vector embeddings, we also experiment with contextualized ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) embedding with the DA model using the version implemented in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017) .

text: Graph neural networks (GNNs) (Scarselli et al., 2009) seek to learn a latent-state representation for each node in a graph by aggregating local information from neighboring nodes and the edges that connect them. Prior research has investigated various mechanisms for combining this local information, as in GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2017), gated graph neural networks (GGNNs) (Li et al., 2016), and graph attention networks (GATs) (Veličković et al., 2018). Several studies have used GNNs in place of Seq2Seq models for text generation (Beck et al., 2018; Marcheggiani and Perez-Beltrachini, 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and some report better performance than Seq2Seq models. However, Damonte and Cohen (2019) employ both kinds of encoders and demonstrate that a GCN can help an LSTM model capture reentrant structures and long-range dependencies, although on a different task from ours. Our approach likewise uses both encoder types, but rather than having one support the other, it integrates them at the same time to exploit their complementary contributions.
source_text: Graph neural networks (GNN) (Scarselli et al., 2009) aim to learn a latent state representation for each node in a graph by aggregating local information from its neighbors and the connected edges. Previous work has explored different ways of aggregating this local information, such as in GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2017), gated graph neural networks (GGNNs) (Li et al., 2016) , and Graph attention networks (GANs) (Veličković et al., 2018) Several works have applied GNNs instead of Seq2Seq models for text generation (Beck et al., 2018; Marcheggiani and Perez-Beltrachini, 2018; Guo et al., 2019; , and some of them outperform Seq2Seq models. However, Damonte and Cohen (2019) use both types of encoders and show that GCN can help LSTM capture reentrant structures and long-range dependencies, albeit on a different problem than ours. Our method also uses the two types of encoders but instead of using one to assist the other, it combines them simultaneously to capture their complementary effects.

text: We now examine attachment preferences in English. The models were trained on English Wikipedia. We evaluated the attachment preferences of the RNN language models using both the original stimuli from Fernández (2003) and a larger stimulus set, in order to obtain a better characterization of model behavior across a broader range of examples. For reasons of space, we report here only the results for the EXTENDED DATA set, the larger stimulus collection, although comparable patterns are observed for the Fernández (2003) stimuli (see Supplemental Materials).
source_text: We turn now to model attachment preferences in English. We trained the models using English Wikipedia. We tested the attachment preferences of the RNN LMs using the original stimuli from Fernández (2003) , and using a larger set of stimuli to have a better sense of model behavior on a wider range of stimuli. For space considerations, we only report here results of the EXTENDED DATA (the larger set of stimuli), but similar results hold for the Fernández (2003) stimuli (see Supplemental Materials).

text: Automatic summarization has attracted renewed attention in recent years, owing to the success of contemporary neural network–based methods (Cheng and Lapata, 2016; Nallapati et al., 2016; Nallapati et al., 2017; Zheng and Lapata, 2019) and the availability of large-scale datasets with hundreds of thousands of document-summary pairs (Sandhaus, 2008; Hermann et al., 2015; Grusky et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu and Lapata, 2019). To date, most research has focused on summarizing news articles, which are typically relatively short and highly formulaic, following an “inverted pyramid” organization that presents the most essential, novel, and interesting elements of a story first, followed by supporting material and secondary details. This rigid structure is advantageous because important passages can be located in predictable positions—for example, by carrying out a “smart selection” of sentences from the beginning of the document—and because the structure itself can be explicitly incorporated into model design, for instance by encoding the relative and absolute position of each sentence.
source_text: Automatic summarization has enjoyed renewed interest in recent years thanks to the popularity of modern neural network-based approaches (Cheng and Lapata, 2016; Nallapati et al., 2016 Nallapati et al., , 2017 Zheng and Lapata, 2019) and the availability of large-scale datasets containing hundreds of thousands of document-summary pairs (Sandhaus, 2008; Hermann et al., 2015; Grusky et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu and Lapata, 2019) . Most efforts to date have concentrated on the summarization of news articles which tend to be relatively short and formulaic following an "inverted pyramid" structure which places the most essential, novel and interesting el- ements of a story in the beginning and supporting material and secondary details afterwards. The rigid structure of news articles is expedient since important passages can be identified in predictable locations (e.g., by performing a "smart selection" of sentences from the beginning of the document) and the structure itself can be explicitly taken into account in model design (e.g., by encoding the relative and absolute position of each sentence).

text: For the error analysis, we select several failed PPVAE examples in Table 9. We divide the errors into two principal categories. (1) Grammatical. Grammatical issues are frequent in NLG. As our analysis indicates, such errors can be alleviated by using a deeper encoder and decoder, together with even more unlabeled data for pre-training. (2) Conditional. Conditional errors are especially relevant to us because they fall within our main focus. We select three representative errors and present them in Table 9. In the first sentence, “shocked” is a nuanced word that may convey either positive or negative sentiment depending on context. Therefore, under a greedy decoding strategy, it may be decoded incorrectly into the opposite polarity. We believe that this type of error could be addressed by more sophisticated decoding strategies (e.g., Weighted Decoding (See et al., 2019)). In the second sentence, the length is constrained by the nature of an interrogative sentence. As a linguistic fact, interrogative sentences often contain fewer words than declarative sentences. In the third sentence, we observe an overlap problem between classes. Some topics (e.g., music album) may occur in both business and entertainment news. In a sense, these samples may also be regarded as correctly conditioned, which underscores the importance of fine-grained human evaluation for this task.
source_text: For the error analysis, we pick some failed examples of PPVAE in Table 9 . We categorize the errors into two main classes. (1) Grammatical. Grammatical problems are common in NLG. As we analyze, this kind of errors can be mitigated with a deeper encoder and decoder with even more unlabeled data for pre-training. (2) Conditional. Conditional errors are of great interest to us since they lie in our focus. We choose three typical errors and list them in Table 9 . In the first sentence, "shocked" is a subtle word which may indicate either positive or negative sentiment depending on the context. Thus, with a greedy decoding strategy, it may be incorrectly decoded into the other polarity. We believe this kind of errors could be fixed with more elaborate decoding strategies (e.g., Weighted Decoding (See et al., 2019) ). In the second sentence, the length is limited by the nature of an interrogative sentence. As a linguistic fact, an interrogative sentence often has fewer words than a declarative sentence. In the third sentence, we remark an overlapping problem between classes. Some topics (e.g., music album) may appear in both business and entertainment news. In some way, these samples can also be considered as correctly conditioned ones, which highlights the importance of a fine-grained human evaluation on this task.

text: Other studies have highlighted the importance of characters and their development for story comprehension (Bamman et al., 2013, 2014; Chaturvedi et al., 2017; Iyyer et al., 2016) and for summarisation (Gorinski and Lapata, 2018). Another key component of narrative organisation is plot, that is, the ordering of events through which characters interact. Neural approaches have explicitly represented events (Martin et al., 2018; Harrison et al., 2017; Rashkin et al., 2018) or the outcomes of actions (Roemmele and Gordon, 2018; Liu et al., 2018a,b). By contrast, some neural generation models (Fan et al., 2018) simply employ a hierarchical model on top of a language model; our architecture adopts this strategy.
source_text: Other work emphasises the role of characters and their development in story understanding (Bamman et al., 2014 (Bamman et al., , 2013 Chaturvedi et al., 2017; Iyyer et al., 2016) or summarisation (Gorinski and Lapata, 2018) . A further important element of narrative structure is plot, i.e., the sequence of events in which characters interact. Neural models have explicitly modelled events (Martin et al., 2018; Harrison et al., 2017; Rashkin et al., 2018) or the results of actions (Roemmele and Gordon, 2018; Liu et al., 2018a,b) . On the other hand, some neural generation models (Fan et al., 2018) just use a hierarchical model on top of a language model; our architecture follows this approach.

text: This paper investigates how the pretraining advantages for downstream tasks vary as the number of training samples grows. To address this question, we concentrate on multi-class text classification because: (i) it is one of the most important problems in NLP, with applications across multiple domains; and (ii) large amounts of training data are available for many text classification tasks, or can be collected at relatively low cost through crowd workers (Snow et al., 2008). We select three sentiment classification datasets: Yelp review (yel, 2019) and Amazon sports and electronics reviews (Ni et al., 2019), with dataset sizes ranging from 6 to 18 million examples. 2 We fine-tune a RoBERTa model with progressively larger portions of the downstream dataset and evaluate performance at each increment. For instance, on the Yelp dataset, which contains 6 million examples, we train models on subsets whose sizes follow the sequence (60k, 600K, 1.8M, 3M, .., 6M). For comparison, we also train a standard BiLSTM and another BiLSTM that uses pretrained RoBERTa token embeddings. We find that when both models are trained on 1% of the data, the performance gap between BiLSTM and RoBERTa is largest, but as the training set size increases, BiLSTM accuracy continues to improve whereas RoBERTa accuracy remains largely unchanged. As the dataset grows, the accuracy gap narrows to within 1%.
source_text: This paper examines the pretraining benefits of downstream tasks as the number of training samples increases. To answer this question, we focus on multi-class text classification since: (i) it is one of most important problems in NLP with applications spanning multiple domains. (ii) large sums of training data exists for many text classification tasks, or can be obtained relatively cheaply through crowd workers (Snow et al., 2008) . We choose three sentiment classification datasets: Yelp review (yel, 2019), Amazon sports and electronics review (Ni et al., 2019) , ranging in size from 6 to 18 million examples. 2 We finetune a RoBERTa model ) with increments of the downstream dataset, and evaluate the performance at each increment. For example, on the Yelp dataset whose size is 6 million, we train the models on subsets of the data with each subset size being in the sequence (60k, 600K, 1.8M, 3M .., 6M). For comparison, we also train a vanilla BiLSTM, and another BiLSTM which uses pretrained Roberta token embeddings. We observe that when both models are trained on 1% of the data, the gap between BiLSTM and RoBERTa models is at its peak, but as the training dataset size increases, the BiLSTM model accuracy keeps on increasing whereas RoBERTa's accuracy remain mostly flat. As the dataset size increases, the accuracy gap shrinks to within 1%.

text: In the setting of structured prediction for natural language processing, active learning has been investigated both for eliciting complete structured outputs (e.g. Thompson et al., 1999; Culotta and Mc-Callum, 2005; Hachey et al., 2005) and for eliciting only parts of outputs (e.g. Ringger et al., 2007; Bloodgood and Callison-Burch, 2010). For sequence labeling tasks, Haertel et al. (2008) showed that annotation effort depends on both the number of words labeled, which we model, and a fixed reading cost, which we do not model.
source_text: In the context of structured prediction for natural language processing, active learning has been considered both for requesting full structured outputs (e.g. Thompson et al., 1999; Culotta and Mc-Callum, 2005; Hachey et al., 2005) and for requesting only pieces of outputs (e.g. Ringger et al., 2007; Bloodgood and Callison-Burch, 2010) . For sequence labeling tasks, Haertel et al. (2008) found that labeling effort depends both on the number of words labeled (which we model), plus a fixed cost for reading (which we do not).
